Distinct from topic (the subject in a phrase) and from thesis statement (the sentence-level expression). AP English Lang FRQ Question 3 (argument, 6 points, 40 minutes) and AP English Lit FRQ Question 3 (open, 8 points) both grade thesis quality directly - rubric upper-range scores require defensible thesis that establishes a line of reasoning; lower-range descriptors include 'restates the prompt' and 'no defensible thesis.'

questions.prompt: Aristotle's three rhetorical appeals are ethos, pathos, and this.
questions.explanation: Logos is Aristotle's third rhetorical appeal (alongside ethos and pathos) in the Rhetoric (c. 4th century BCE) - the appeal to logic, reasoning, and evidence. Logos operates through enthymemes (compressed syllogisms), examples (inductive evidence), facts, statistics, expert testimony, and chains of reasoning. Distinct from ethos (speaker's character) and pathos (audience emotion). Effective persuasion typically integrates all three appeals rather than relying on one. NYS RI.11-12.6 covers analyzing how authors use rhetoric to advance point of view and purpose. AP English Lang FRQ Question 2 (rhetorical analysis, 6 points) routinely tests logos identification - rubric upper-range awards integration of logos with ethos and pathos analysis.

questions.prompt: The opposing view a writer must acknowledge fairly and thoroughly is this.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is the opposing view the writer must fairly acknowledge in argumentative writing. NYS W.11-12.1 explicitly requires students to 'develop claim(s) and counterclaim(s) fairly and thoroughly, supplying the most relevant evidence for each while pointing out the strengths and limitations of both.' The technique strengthens the writer's ethos (intellectual honesty), neutralizes anticipated objections, and demonstrates engagement with the live debate. Distinct from concession (briefly granting validity) and refutation (disproving). AP English Lang FRQ Question 3 (argument, 6 points) rubric awards upper-range scores (5-6) only to essays that engage opposing views, not just one-sided assertion. Mere mention without engagement scores in the 3-4 middle range.

questions.prompt: Logical errors like ad hominem and straw man are collectively called these.
questions.explanation: Logical fallacies are errors in reasoning that undermine arguments - the classical taxonomy goes back to Aristotle's Sophistical Refutations (c. 4th century BCE). Major categories: ad hominem (attacking the person), straw man (misrepresenting opposing view), post hoc (assuming temporal sequence proves causation), slippery slope (assuming first step entails disaster), false dilemma (presenting only two options), appeal to false authority, circular reasoning, hasty generalization. NYS RI.11-12.8 explicitly requires students to 'delineate and evaluate the reasoning in seminal U.S. texts... including the application of constitutional principles and use of legal reasoning.' AP English Lang FRQ Question 2 (rhetorical analysis, 6 points) rewards fallacy identification.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's framework of writer, audience, and subject forms this rhetorical figure.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical triangle is Aristotle's framework (Rhetoric, c. 4th century BCE) of speaker/writer, audience, and subject - the three corners of every rhetorical situation, with the three Aristotelian appeals corresponding to each corner: ethos (speaker, character/credibility), pathos (audience, emotion), logos (subject, logic/evidence). All rhetorical analysis builds on the triangle. NYS RI.11-12.6 covers analyzing how authors use rhetoric; the AP English Language and Composition curriculum framework rests on the rhetorical triangle. AP English Lang FRQ Question 2 (rhetorical analysis, 6 points) implicitly grades students' ability to analyze how speakers balance the three appeals to fit their specific speaker-audience-subject configuration - upper-range essays integrate all three corners.

questions.prompt: Demonstrating a character is angry through action rather than telling readers uses this principle.
questions.explanation: 'Show, don't tell' is the foundational creative-writing principle of preferring dramatized action over labeled emotion - demonstrating that a character is angry through clenched fists, sharp dialogue, slammed doors, rather than stating 'she was angry.' The principle prioritizes the reader's inference over the writer's declaration, producing immediacy and respecting reader intelligence. Henry James's Notebooks (1890s) advocated dramatized scene over summary; modern creative-writing pedagogy (Janet Burroway's Writing Fiction 1982, John Gardner's The Art of Fiction 1983) systematized the principle. NYS W.11-12.3 covers narrative writing with 'sensory language to convey a vivid picture' - the standard's preference for sensory dramatization over summary statement.

questions.prompt: Restating an author's idea in your own words while preserving meaning is this.
questions.explanation: Paraphrase is the rhetorical move of restating an author's idea in your own words while preserving meaning, length, and source attribution. Distinct from summary (compresses), direct quotation (preserves exact words), and plagiarism (uncited borrowing of either ideas or words). Effective paraphrase demonstrates the writer's comprehension AND integrates ideas smoothly into the writer's argument. Always cite paraphrase - unattributed paraphrase is plagiarism even when the wording differs from source. NYS W.11-12.8 covers gathering relevant information from multiple sources, 'integrating information into the text selectively to maintain the flow of ideas, avoiding plagiarism.' AP English Lang FRQ Question 1 (synthesis, 6 points) rewards paraphrase integration.

questions.prompt: The deliberate arrangement of words for effect, including sentence length and structure, is this.
questions.explanation: Syntax is the deliberate arrangement of words and phrases into sentences - the order, structure, and grammatical relationships that produce meaning beyond individual words. Distinct from diction (word choice) and grammar (rules). Syntactic variety controls rhythm, emphasis, and reader experience: short sentences punch, long sentences develop. Hemingway's paratactic style (short, declarative, coordinate) produces apparent neutrality; Henry James's hypotactic style (long, subordinate, qualified) produces nuanced reflection. NYS L.11-12.3 covers using language for effect; RL.11-12.4 covers analyzing impact of structure on meaning and tone. AP English Lang FRQ Question 2 (rhetorical analysis, 6 points) and AP English Lit FRQ Question 1 (poetry, 6 points) reward syntax analysis.

questions.prompt: Miller's 1949 drama of Willy Loman's tragic delusions about success.
questions.explanation: Arthur Miller's Death of a Salesman (1949) creates the modern tragic hero in Willy Loman, the failing 63-year-old traveling salesman whose expressionist flashbacks to brother Ben, the Boston hotel room and Biff's discovery, and the planted carrot seeds dramatize hamartia (self-deception). Miller's essay 'Tragedy and the Common Man' (Feb 1949 New York Times) explicitly argued that the everyman can achieve tragic stature. The 'Requiem' ('He had the wrong dreams. All, all wrong') delivers the verdict; Biff's 'He never knew who he was' confirms the failure. NYS RL.11-12.3 covers character; RL.11-12.5 covers structure. AP English Lit FRQ Question 3 (open, 8 points) regularly accepts Salesman.

questions.prompt: The speed and rhythm of narrative time is called this.
questions.explanation: Pacing is the deliberate manipulation of narrative time - how the writer expands some moments into extended scenes, summarizes other stretches, and skips still others through ellipsis - to control emphasis, rhythm, and reader experience. Gerard Genette (Narrative Discourse 1972) systematized pacing into four time-rate modes: scene (real-time dramatized action), summary (compressed days into paragraphs), pause (description halts action), and ellipsis (narrative skips entirely). NYS RL.11-12.5 covers analyzing how authors structure parts of a text; W.11-12.3 covers narrative writing techniques. AP English Lit FRQ Question 2 (prose passage, 6 points) and Question 3 (open, 8 points) reward pacing analysis as deliberate authorial structuring.

questions.prompt: Shakespeare's 14-line poem with abab cdcd efef gg rhyme is called this.
questions.explanation: The Shakespearean (English) sonnet is fourteen lines of iambic pentameter rhyming abab cdcd efef gg, structured as three quatrains developing the argument and a closing couplet delivering the volta (turn) and resolution. Henry Howard, Earl of Surrey, adapted the Italian form for English in the 1540s; Shakespeare published his 154-sonnet sequence in 1609 (Sonnets to a Young Man, Sonnets to a Dark Lady). Sonnet 18 ('Shall I compare thee to a summer's day') and Sonnet 130 ('My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun') are canonical. NYS RL.11-12.5 covers form-meaning relationships. AP English Lit FRQ Question 1 (poetry analysis, 6 points) routinely tests sonnet form - rubric rewards scansion, volta identification, and form-content integration.

questions.prompt: Shakespeare's unrhymed iambic pentameter in his plays is called this verse form.
questions.explanation: Blank verse is unrhymed iambic pentameter, ten syllables per line in alternating unstressed/stressed pattern. Henry Howard, Earl of Surrey, introduced it to English with his 1557 translation of Virgil's Aeneid Books 2 and 4. Shakespeare made it the standard meter of English verse drama, using it for noble characters and serious matter while reserving prose for servants, comic scenes, and madness. Milton's Paradise Lost (1667), Wordsworth's The Prelude (1799-1850), and Tennyson's Idylls of the King are extended blank-verse narratives. NYS RL.11-12.5 covers form. AP English Lit FRQ Question 1 (poetry analysis, 6 points) tests scansion of blank verse and identification of metrical departures that signal meaning.

questions.prompt: Anglo-Saxon poetry's structural feature, audible in Beowulf, is the caesura combined with this.
questions.explanation: Anglo-Saxon (Old English) poetry's metrical foundation is the alliterative line: two half-lines (hemistichs) separated by a caesura (medial pause), linked by alliteration on stressed syllables across the caesura. Beowulf's opening 'Hwaet! We Gardena in geardagum, / theodcyninga, thrym gefrunon' carries 'g' alliteration ('Gardena... geardagum') and 'th' alliteration ('theodcyninga... thrym'). Each line has four stressed syllables (two per half-line). Seamus Heaney's 2000 translation preserves the device for modern readers. NYS RL.11-12.4 covers diction and sound; RL.11-12.9 covers Anglo-Saxon literary tradition. AP English Lit FRQ Question 1 (poetry, 6 points) occasionally tests the alliterative line on Beowulf passages.

questions.prompt: The metrical pattern of unstressed-stressed syllables in five pairs per line is called this.
questions.explanation: Iambic pentameter is English's most common meter: five iambs per line, each iamb consisting of one unstressed followed by one stressed syllable (x /). The pattern produces the foundational rhythm of English verse drama and verse narrative from Chaucer through Shakespeare to Milton, Pope, and Wordsworth. Shakespeare uses iambic pentameter in 154 sonnets and 38+ plays; Milton in Paradise Lost (1667, blank verse - unrhymed iambic pentameter); Chaucer in Canterbury Tales (rhyming couplets). NYS RL.11-12.4 covers diction and rhythm; RL.11-12.5 covers form. AP English Lit FRQ Question 1 (poetry analysis, 6 points) demands scansion and identification of metrical substitutions (trochees, spondees) at meaning-bearing moments.

questions.prompt: Chaucer's Canterbury Tales uses this meter (10 syllables per line, paired couplets).
questions.explanation: Geoffrey Chaucer's The Canterbury Tales (c. 1387-1400) uses iambic pentameter rhyming couplets (aa bb cc dd...), a form sometimes called heroic couplets in its later Augustan refinement. Chaucer's General Prologue ('Whan that Aprille with his shoures soote') and most of the twenty-four tales employ the form, which became foundational for English versification. Dryden, Pope (The Rape of the Lock 1712, An Essay on Criticism 1711), and Samuel Johnson refined the closed heroic couplet through the 18th century. NYS RL.11-12.5 covers form-meaning relationships. AP English Lit FRQ Question 1 (poetry, 6 points) rewards students who recognize how couplet structure enables epigrammatic compression and witty antithesis.
